1: The historical anchor: Sargon king of Assyria -- the only occurrence of Sargon's name in the Bible, confirmed by Assyrian annals for the capture of Ashdod in 711 BC. The commander-in-chief (tartan) is an Assyrian military title preserved in Hebrew transliteration. Melek (king, disp 2) -- Sargon as king is the flagged usage; the MKT's king of Assyria is standard. The precision of the date-formula is a claim of specific historical grounding for what follows as prophetic sign-act.
2: YHWH is flagged -- the divine name as the one who speaks through (beyad: by the hand of) Isaiah. The command to Isaiah is stark: remove sackcloth and sandals and walk naked. Sackcloth was the prophetic garment, identifying Isaiah's public prophetic identity. The command strips him of his identity-garb to embody the stripping of Egypt and Cush. The MKT's he obeyed makes Isaiah's compliance explicit from the Hebrew verbal form.
3: YHWH is flagged -- the divine name confirms the command and its duration: three years as a sign and portent (ot u-mofet). Sign and portent is the paired phrase of prophetic symbolic action (Deuteronomy 28:46; Ezek 4-5). Three years of nakedness is an extraordinary commitment to prophetic embodiment. The word portent (mofet) means wonder or omen -- Isaiah's naked body is an omen of what is coming to Egypt and Cush.
4: Melek (king, disp 2) -- the king of Assyria who will lead away the Egyptian captives. The sign is interpreted: naked and barefoot Isaiah represents the captives who will be stripped and marched out. The Hebrew erom (naked) is intensified by specifying buttocks bared -- the humiliation of captivity described with anatomical frankness the oracle requires. The MKT preserves this without euphemism.
5: Nations that had relied on Cush and boasted about Egypt will find their guarantor stripped and captive. The language of hope shifted to terror and shame -- the standard prophetic reversal of misplaced confidence. Egypt trusted in idols (Isaiah 19); nations trusted in Egypt; now both the idols and Egypt fail together.
6: Melek (king, disp 2) -- the coastland peoples realize their flight to Egypt as protection from the king of Assyria has failed. The oracle closes with the question: if Egypt -- the one we escaped to -- has been captured, how shall we ourselves escape? The sign-act (three years of nakedness) has compressed a multi-year geopolitical process into bodily demonstration.
